ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for potato chips as a food product; SNOMED CT coverage for processed snack foods is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve snack portion of potato chips is approximately 30 g, equivalent to roughly one small bag or a small handful.

nutrient_source.title: Potato chips, plain, salted — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for plain salted potato chips. All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95 as recorded in the input per_100g_meta.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 85 for plain salted potato chips is consistent with values reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables (Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283). Glycemic load per 30 g serving calculated as: (85 × 53.4 × 0.30) / 100 = 13.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 547 mg per 100 g; a 30 g serving delivers approximately 164 mg sodium, and typical consumption often exceeds one serving
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit potato chips due to the high sodium content (547 mg/100 g), which contributes meaningfully to daily sodium load and can elevate blood pressure; lower-sodium baked alternatives are preferable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 85) combined with 53.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g produces a high glycemic load, causing rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid potato chips because the combination of a very high GI (85) and a large carbohydrate load (53.4 g/100 g) causes rapid and pronounced postprandial glucose spikes that are difficult to manage; the 3.3 g dietary fibre is insufficient to buffer this glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 25.1 g/100 g including saturated fat ~6.5 g/100 g and trans fat ~0.2 g/100 g, combined with high sodium 547 mg/100 g and ultra-processed deep-fried preparation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid potato chips due to the combination of high saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium, all of which adversely affect lipid profiles and blood pressure; deep-frying also generates pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products (AGEs).

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates AGEs; trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g) and saturated fat (~6.5 g/100 g) promote inflammatory cytokine production; potato is a nightshade vegetable containing solanine
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid potato chips because deep-frying generates AGEs that exacerbate joint inflammation, trans fat and saturated fat promote TNF-alpha and IL-6 cytokine production, and solanine from potato (a nightshade) may trigger flares in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Potato chips are a globally popular ultra-processed snack food made by thinly slicing raw potatoes and deep-frying them in vegetable oil until crispy, then seasoning with salt. The result is a light, crunchy, golden-brown snack with a distinctly salty flavour and a characteristic oily richness. Per 100 g, potato chips provide approximately 463 kcal, 6 g protein, 25.1 g fat (including ~6.5 g saturated fat and ~0.2 g trans fat), 53.4 g carbohydrate, 4.2 g sugar, 3.3 g dietary fibre, and 547 mg sodium. The glycemic index is high (GI 85), making them unsuitable for blood glucose management. The deep-frying process generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that contribute to systemic inflammation. Clinically, potato chips are of high concern for hypertension (high sodium), type 2 diabetes (high GI and carbohydrate load), cardiovascular disease (saturated fat, trans fat, sodium), and rheumatoid arthritis (AGEs, nightshade solanine, pro-inflammatory fats). They are vegetarian, vegan, and typically gluten-free, but are not recommended as a regular dietary component for any health-conscious eating pattern. Common serving size is approximately 30 g (one small bag or a small handful).
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in potato chips?
Are potato chips bad for high blood pressure?
Can I eat crisps if I have rheumatoid arthritis?

confidence_reason: Core macros and secondary nutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database at confidence 0.95. GI value of 85 is consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed international GI tables. Saturated fat (~6.5 g/100 g), trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g), and potassium (~480 mg/100 g) are cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for plain salted potato chips (FDC ID 19411).
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown (MUFA/PUFA split) is not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for this item; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 19411) reports MUFA ~6.1 g and PUFA ~11.0 g per 100 g for plain salted potato chips, but these values are not directly confirmed in the HPB source and are therefore not adopted into per_100g to preserve source consistency. water_g is set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source for this entry. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for this food item. trans_fat_g of 0.2 g/100 g and saturated_fat_g of 6.5 g/100 g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 19411) as the HPB source does not disaggregate fat fractions; this estimation is documented here. potassium_mg of 480 mg/100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 19411) as HPB does not report potassium for this entry. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — potato chips fried in vegetable oil contain no cholesterol). The input dietary_tags included 'high_fibre' which is not supported: 3.3 g dietary fibre per 100 g does not meet the high-fibre threshold (≥6 g/100 g per Singapore HPB guidelines); this tag has been removed. GI glycemic load per serving calculated as (85 × 53.4 × 0.30) / 100 = 13.6 for a 30 g serving.
